text: Welcome to Dr. Andre Teixeira's YouTube channel. Today we will be watching Dr. Teixeira perform a gastric sleeve, also known as a sleeve gastrectomy, along with a hiatal hernia repair. As we watch Dr. Teixeira separating the omentum, or fat tissue from the stomach, let's discuss both procedures. Hiatal hernias are different from other abdominal hernias because they do not involve tissue pushing through a defect in the fascia of the abdomen. Rather, the upper portion of the stomach is now pushing through the diaphragm and into the chest cavity.

Most obese patients develop a hiatal hernia during their lives and a bariatric procedure is the perfect time to repair these as well. This will reduce the likelihood of long-term gastroesophageal reflux disease, also known as GERD, while also minimizing the risks of a paraesophageal hernia or when the stomach becomes lodged next to the esophagus. Hiatal hernia repairs are very straightforward and very effective. During a consultation with Dr. Teixeira, you will learn more about hiatal hernia and whether Dr. Teixeira expects to repair one during your procedure. The addition of the hiatal hernia repair does not change the post-op recovery and long-term bariatric lifestyle.

Of note, Dr. Teixeira is using robotic assistance to perform these surgeries. The surgical robot does not perform any of this procedure by itself. Rather, Dr. Teixeira is fully in control of the robotic arms while comfortably seated at a console to the side of the operating table. Robotic bariatric surgery is a revolutionary step forward from traditional laparoscopy. The robot uses advanced, three-D camera technology to allow Dr. Teixeira to view the abdominal cavity as if he were fully immersed in the surgical field. Gone are the days of using the two-dimensional laparoscopy TVs above the patient. Further, you may have noticed that the robotic arms have wristed hinges. This allows them to move three hundred and sixty degrees inside of the abdomen. You can liken it to having your own hands inside of the abdomen, but only needing tiny incisions to access it. This is a huge leap forward, offering the best of open surgery and traditional laparoscopy rolled into one. Surgeon fatigue also plays a role. We have a very high volume of bariatric patients, as many patients seek our expertise. Using traditional laparoscopy, we would not be able to perform as many procedures, which would mean that patients would have longer wait times to receive their surgery. The robot truly has revolutionized our practice and the surgical field as a whole.

With that being said, robotic surgery is not the only option. Many straightforward procedures can be performed just as safely and effectively using traditional laparoscopy. However, robotic surgery does offer a number of clear benefits that make it a staple in Dr. Teixeira's practice. The gastric sleeve is the most popular bariatric surgery in the United States. It is considered a purely restrictive procedure, which means that it simply reduces the amount of food that a patient can eat at any given sitting. During the procedure, Dr. Teixeira will cut away approximately eighty-five percent of the stomach, leaving a banana or sleeve-shaped stomach pouch behind. The remaining portion of the stomach works in similar fashion to the pre-surgery stomach anatomy because the inlet and outlet of the stomach are not altered as they are with the gastric bypass.

While the main mechanism for weight loss during a gastric sleeve is making the stomach smaller and reducing the number of calories in, there's also a hormonal benefit to the procedure. The portion of the stomach that is cut away is removed from the abdomen. This portion of stomach contains the majority of the production of ghrelin, known as the hunger hormone. By removing this part of the stomach, most patients also experience fewer hunger pangs after surgery. It is important to know that while bariatric surgery remains major surgery, technologies such as the surgical robot that you see on the screen, as well as innovative surgical technique, have improved to a degree that bariatric surgery is no more dangerous than a simple cholecystectomy. We hope to see you in our office soon to discuss the possibility of bariatric surgery. You can also visit Dr. Teixeira on Instagram and Facebook to see what's going on in the practice day in and day out.
